records.ety: [L. enseigne, L. insignia, pl. of insigne a distinctive mark, badge, flag; in + signum mark, sign. See Sign, and cf. Insignia, 3d Ancient.]

defs: A flag; a banner; a standard; esp., the national flag, or a banner indicating nationality, carried by a ship or a body of soldiers; -- as distinguished from flags indicating divisions of the army, rank of naval officers, or private signals, and the like.
quotes: Hang up your ensigns, let your drums be still.

defs: A signal displayed like a standard, to give notice.
quotes: He will lift an ensign to the nations from far.

defs: Sign; badge of office, rank, or power; symbol.
quotes: The ensigns of our power about we bear.

defs: Formerly, a commissioned officer of the army who carried the ensign or flag of a company or regiment.
A commissioned officer of the lowest grade in the navy, corresponding to the grade of second lieutenant in the army.
notes: ☞ In the British army the rank of ensign was abolished in 1871. In the United States army the rank is not recognized; the regimental flags being carried by a sergeant called the color sergeant.

defs: To designate as by an ensign.
quotes: Henry but joined the roses that ensigned Particular families.

defs: To distinguish by a mark or ornament; esp. (Her.), by a crown; thus, any charge which has a crown immediately above or upon it, is said to be ensigned.
